20	)

22	// Exit codes for ddx try terminal outcomes. These are specific to ddx try
23	// and intentionally do not reuse the generic ExitCode* constants because
24	// the bead-execution outcome vocabulary is distinct from generic CLI errors.
25	const (
26		tryExitSuccess         = 0 // success or already_satisfied
27		tryExitPreservedOrNone = 1 // preserved_for_review or no_changes
28		tryExitFailed          = 2 // execution_failed or any other terminal non-success
29	)
30	
31	// newTryCommand creates the top-level "ddx try <bead-id>" command.
32	// It targets a single specific bead through the full production execution
33	// machinery (claim → execute → land → review), unlike "ddx work" which drains
34	// the queue by picker priority and cannot force a specific bead.
35	func (f *CommandFactory) newTryCommand() *cobra.Command {
36		cmd := &cobra.Command{
37			Use:          "try <bead-id>",
38			Short:        "Execute a specific bead through the production machinery",
39			SilenceUsage: true,
40			Long: `try executes a single named bead through the full production
41	execution machinery: claim → execute → land → review.
42	
43	Unlike "ddx work" which drains the queue by picker priority, "ddx try"
44	targets a specific bead ID. This is useful for:
45	  - Operator-driven debugging when a bead misbehaves under autonomous drain
46	  - Deterministic queue ordering during refactor execution (dispatch each child
47	    in dep order with an explicit ID)
48	  - Path-comparison studies that need to execute one fixture bead at a time
49	
50	Pre-flight checks (run before any claim is issued):
51	  - bead not found → non-zero exit with "bead not found: <id>"
52	  - bead not claimable (status=closed, cancelled, etc.) → non-zero exit
53	  - bead has unmet dependencies → non-zero exit listing the blockers
54	
55	Review is on by default. --no-review is a break-glass override and
56	requires --no-review-i-know-what-im-doing. A bead label of review:skip
57	is only honored when it also carries a sibling review:skip-reason:*
58	label; otherwise the label is ignored.
59	
60	Flag plumbing is identical to "ddx work": --harness, --model, --provider,
61	--profile, --effort are forwarded to the agent as opaque passthrough
62	constraints. DDx does not validate or branch on their string values.
63	
64	Exit codes:
65	  0  success or already_satisfied
66	  1  preserved_for_review or no_changes
67	  2  execution_failed or any other terminal non-success
68	`,
69			Example: `  # Execute a specific bead
70	  ddx try ddx-abc12345
71	
72	  # Execute with an explicit harness and model
73	  ddx try ddx-abc12345 --harness codex --model gpt-5.4-mini
74	
75	  # Execute without the post-merge review step (break-glass only)
76	  ddx try ddx-abc12345 --no-review --no-review-i-know-what-im-doing`,
77			Args: cobra.ExactArgs(1),
78			RunE: f.runTry,
79		}
80	
81		// Routing knobs — identical to "ddx work" passthrough surface.
82		cmd.Flags().String("project", "", "Target project root path or name (default: CWD git root). Env: DDX_PROJECT_ROOT")
83		cmd.Flags().String("from", "", "Base git revision to start from (default: HEAD)")
84		cmd.Flags().String("harness", "", "Agent harness constraint (passthrough; ddx try does not validate)")
85		cmd.Flags().String("model", "", "Model constraint (passthrough; ddx try does not validate)")
86		cmd.Flags().String("profile", agent.DefaultRoutingProfile, "Routing profile: default, cheap, fast, or smart")
87		cmd.Flags().String("provider", "", "Provider constraint (passthrough; ddx try does not validate)")
88		cmd.Flags().String("model-ref", "", "Model catalog reference (e.g. code-medium); resolved via the model catalog")
89		cmd.Flags().String("effort", "", "Effort level")
90		cmd.Flags().Bool("no-review", false, "Skip post-merge review (break-glass: requires --no-review-i-know-what-im-doing)")
91		cmd.Flags().Bool("no-review-i-know-what-im-doing", false, "Break-glass acknowledgement required when using --no-review")
92		cmd.Flags().String("review-harness", "", "Harness for the post-merge reviewer (default: same as implementation harness)")
93		cmd.Flags().String("review-model", "", "Model override for the post-merge reviewer (default: smart tier)")
94		cmd.Flags().Duration("request-timeout", 0, "Per-request provider wall-clock timeout; overrides project config and model-class defaults")
95		cmd.Flags().Int("min-power", 0, "Minimum model power required (0 = unconstrained)")
96		cmd.Flags().Int("max-power", 0, "Maximum model power allowed (0 = unconstrained)")
97	
98		return cmd
99	}
100	
101	// runTry is the RunE for "ddx try <bead-id>".
102	func (f *CommandFactory) runTry(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
103		beadID := args[0]
104	
105		projectFlag, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("project")
106		projectRoot := resolveProjectRoot(projectFlag, f.WorkingDir)
107		fromRev, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("from")
108		harness, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("harness")
109		model, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("model")
110		profile, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("profile")
111		provider, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("provider")
112		modelRef, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("model-ref")
113		effort, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("effort")
114		noReview, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-review")
115		noReviewAck, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-review-i-know-what-im-doing")
116		reviewHarness, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("review-harness")
117		reviewModel, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("review-model")
118		requestTimeout, _ := cmd.Flags().GetDuration("request-timeout")
119		minPower, _ := cmd.Flags().GetInt("min-power")
120		maxPower, _ := cmd.Flags().GetInt("max-power")
121	
122		if noReview && !noReviewAck {
123			return fmt.Errorf("--no-review requires --no-review-i-know-what-im-doing (break-glass acknowledgement)")
124		}
125	
126		store := bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx"))
127	
128		// Pre-flight: look up the bead.
129		target, err := store.Get(beadID)
130		if err != nil {
131			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "bead not found: %s\n", beadID)
132			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
133		}
134	
135		// Pre-flight: verify the bead is in a claimable state.
136		// Claimable = open, or stale in_progress (the loop handles stale reclaim).
137		switch target.Status {
138		case bead.StatusOpen, bead.StatusInProgress:
139			// allowed — loop's Claim() handles stale-heartbeat reclaim
140		default:
141			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "bead is not claimable (status=%s)\n", target.Status)
142			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
143		}
144	
145		// Pre-flight: check for unmet dependencies.
146		if unmet := unmetDeps(store, target); len(unmet) > 0 {
147			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "bead has unmet dependencies: %s\n", strings.Join(unmet, ", "))
148			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
149		}
150	
151		// Wrap the real store so that ReadyExecution() returns only the target bead.
152		// All other operations pass through to the underlying store unchanged.
153		forcedStore := &singleBeadStore{Store: store, target: *target}
154	
155		// Set up progress logging (same as runAgentExecuteLoopImpl).
156		loopSessionID := fmt.Sprintf("agent-loop-%d", time.Now().UnixNano())
157		loopLogDir := filepath.Join(projectRoot, agent.DefaultLogDir)
158		_ = os.MkdirAll(loopLogDir, 0o755)
159		loopLogPath := filepath.Join(loopLogDir, loopSessionID+".jsonl")
160		var loopSink io.Writer
161		if lf, openErr := os.OpenFile(loopLogPath, os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY|os.O_APPEND, 0o644); openErr == nil {
162			loopSink = lf
163			defer lf.Close() //nolint:errcheck
164		}
165	
166		// Worker-probe (best-effort server mirror; non-blocking).
167		probeIdent := workerprobe.Identity{
168			ProjectRoot:  projectRoot,
169			Harness:      harness,
170			Model:        model,
171			ExecutorPID:  os.Getpid(),
172			ExecutorHost: hostnameOrEmpty(),
173			StartedAt:    time.Now().UTC(),
174		}
175		probe := workerprobe.New(probeIdent, workerprobe.Config{
176			AddrFunc: serverpkg.ReadServerAddr,
177		})
178		probeCtx, probeCancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
179		probe.Start(probeCtx)
180		defer func() {
181			probeCancel()
182			probe.Stop()
183		}()
184		if loopSink != nil {
185			loopSink = workerprobe.TeeJSONL(loopSink, probe)
186		} else {
187			loopSink = workerprobe.TeeJSONL(io.Discard, probe)
188		}
189	
190		// Process-local LandCoordinator.
191		localCoord := serverpkg.NewLocalLandCoordinator(projectRoot, agent.RealLandingGitOps{})
192		defer localCoord.Stop()
193	
194		// Post-merge reviewer (on by default, skipped via --no-review).
195		var reviewer agent.BeadReviewer
196		if !noReview {
197			reviewer = &agent.DefaultBeadReviewer{
198				ProjectRoot: projectRoot,
199				BeadStore:   bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx")),
200				BeadEvents:  bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx")),
201				Harness:     reviewHarness,
202				Model:       reviewModel,
203			}
204		}
205	
206		profile = agent.NormalizeRoutingProfile(profile)
207	
208		// Determine whether zero-config auto-route applies (same logic as runWork).
209		noRoutingFlags := harness == "" && model == "" && provider == "" && modelRef == "" &&
210			!cmd.Flags().Changed("profile")
211		autoInferTier := noRoutingFlags && !projectHasRoutingConfig(projectRoot)
212		resourceChecker := buildCLIResourceChecker(projectRoot, f.resourceCheckerOverride)
213	
214		// Build the executor: either the test override or the real single-tier executor.
215		var executor agent.ExecuteBeadExecutor
216		if f.tryExecutorOverride != nil {
217			executor = f.tryExecutorOverride
218		} else {
219			executor = agent.ExecuteBeadExecutorFunc(func(ctx context.Context, execBeadID string) (agent.ExecuteBeadReport, error) {
220				var inferredTier escalation.ModelTier
221				if autoInferTier {
222					if b, getErr := store.Get(execBeadID); getErr == nil {
223						inferredTier = escalation.InferTier(b)
224					} else {
225						inferredTier = escalation.TierCheap
226					}
227				}
228	
229				attemptProfile := profile
230				if autoInferTier && inferredTier != "" {
231					attemptProfile = escalation.TierToProfile(inferredTier)
232				}
233	
234				loopOverrides := config.CLIOverrides{
235					Harness:           harness,
236					Model:             model,

246					loopOverrides.ProviderRequestTimeout = &requestTimeout
247				}
248				attemptRcfg, _ := config.LoadAndResolve(projectRoot, loopOverrides)

250				res, execErr := agent.ExecuteBeadWithConfig(ctx, projectRoot, execBeadID, attemptRcfg, agent.ExecuteBeadRuntime{
251					FromRev:         fromRev,
252					Output:          cmd.OutOrStdout(),

254					ResourceChecker: resourceChecker,

256				if execErr != nil && res == nil {
257					return agent.ExecuteBeadReport{}, execErr
258				}
259				if res != nil && agent.IsResourceExhaustedStatus(res.Status) {
260					return agent.ReportFromExecuteBeadResult(res, string(inferredTier)), nil
261				}
262				if execErr != nil {
263					agent.MarkResultExecutionError(res, execErr)
264					return agent.ReportFromExecuteBeadResult(res, string(inferredTier)), nil
265				}
266				if res != nil && res.ResultRev != "" && res.ResultRev != res.BaseRev && res.ExitCode == 0 {
267					targetBead, _ := store.Get(execBeadID)
268					landRes, _, landErr := agent.SubmitWithPreMergeChecks(
269						ctx, projectRoot, targetBead, res,
270						func(req agent.LandRequest) (*agent.LandResult, error) { return localCoord.Submit(req) },
271						bead.NewStore(filepath.Join(projectRoot, ".ddx")),
272						resolveClaimAssignee(), "ddx try",
273						nil,
274					)
275					if landErr == nil {
276						agent.ApplyLandResultToExecuteBeadResult(res, landRes)
277					} else {
278						agent.MarkResultLandError(projectRoot, res, landErr)
279						return agent.ReportFromExecuteBeadResult(res, string(inferredTier)), nil
280					}
281				} else if res != nil && res.ResultRev == res.BaseRev {
282					res.Outcome = "no-changes"
283					res.Status = agent.ClassifyExecuteBeadStatus(res.Outcome, res.ExitCode, res.Reason)
284				} else if res != nil && res.ExitCode != 0 {
285					res.Outcome = "preserved"
286					res.Status = agent.ClassifyExecuteBeadStatus(res.Outcome, res.ExitCode, res.Reason)
287				}
288				tierStr := ""
289				if inferredTier != "" {
290					tierStr = string(inferredTier)
291				}
292				return agent.ReportFromExecuteBeadResult(res, tierStr), nil
293			})
294		}

296		worker := &agent.ExecuteBeadWorker{
297			Store:    forcedStore,
298			Executor: executor,
299			Reviewer: reviewer,
300		}
301	
302		overrides := config.CLIOverrides{
303			Assignee: resolveClaimAssignee(),
304			Harness:  harness,
305			Model:    model,
306			Provider: provider,
307			ModelRef: modelRef,
308			Profile:  profile,
309			Effort:   effort,
310		}
311		rcfg, err := config.LoadAndResolve(projectRoot, overrides)
312		if err != nil {
313			return fmt.Errorf("load resolved config: %w", err)
314		}
315		if _, err := resourceChecker.Check(cmd.Context()); err != nil {
316			if resErr, ok := err.(*agent.ResourceExhaustedError); ok && resErr != nil {
317				fmt.Fprintln(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), resErr.Error())
318			}
319			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: err.Error()}
320		}

322		cliLandingOps := agent.RealLandingGitOps{}
323		var qualityRunner agent.AgentRunner
324		if f.AgentRunnerOverride != nil {
325			qualityRunner = f.AgentRunnerOverride
326		}
327		lintHook := agent.NewPreDispatchLintHook(projectRoot, store, rcfg, nil, qualityRunner)
328		triageHook := agent.NewPostAttemptTriageHook(projectRoot, store, rcfg, nil, qualityRunner, nil)
329		result, runErr := worker.Run(cmd.Context(), rcfg, agent.ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime{
330			Once:                  true,
331			Log:                   cmd.OutOrStdout(),
332			EventSink:             loopSink,
333			WorkerID:              resolveClaimAssignee(),
334			ProjectRoot:           projectRoot,
335			SessionID:             loopSessionID,
336			PreClaimHook:          buildCLIPreClaimHook(projectRoot, cliLandingOps),
337			PreDispatchLintHook:   lintHook,
338			PostAttemptTriageHook: triageHook,
339			NoReview:              noReview,
340		})
341		if runErr != nil {
342			if (errors.Is(runErr, context.Canceled) || errors.Is(runErr, context.DeadlineExceeded)) && result != nil && len(result.Results) > 0 {
343				writeTryResult(cmd.OutOrStdout(), result.Results[0])
344			}
345			return runErr
346		}

348		// Report outcome.
349		if result.NoReadyWork {
350			// Bead was not in the execution-ready queue even after pre-flight passed.
351			// This can happen if a heartbeat re-appeared just before claim, making it
352			// not stale-enough for reclaim. Surface a clear message.
353			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), "bead is not execution-ready (it may be actively claimed by another worker): %s\n", beadID)
354			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
355		}
356	
357		if len(result.Results) == 0 {
358			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
359		}
360		report := result.Results[0]
361		writeTryResult(cmd.OutOrStdout(), report)
362		if intent := escalation.ParseExecutionHint(target); intent.Source == escalation.ExecutionIntentSourceBeadHint && intent.RequestedTier != "" {
363			fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "routing intent: tier=%s source=%s\n", intent.RequestedTier, intent.Source)
364		}
365	
366		return tryExitCodeForStatus(report.Status)
367	}
368	
369	func writeTryResult(w io.Writer, report agent.ExecuteBeadReport) {
370		fmt.Fprintf(w, "\nbead: %s\nstatus: %s\n", report.BeadID, report.Status)
371		if report.Detail != "" {
372			fmt.Fprintf(w, "detail: %s\n", report.Detail)
373		}
374		if report.ResultRev != "" {
375			fmt.Fprintf(w, "result_rev: %s\n", report.ResultRev)
376		}
377		if report.PreserveRef != "" {
378			fmt.Fprintf(w, "preserve_ref: %s\n", report.PreserveRef)
379		}
380	}
381	
382	// tryExitCodeForStatus maps a bead execution status string to the ddx try exit code contract.
383	func tryExitCodeForStatus(status string) error {
384		switch status {
385		case agent.ExecuteBeadStatusSuccess, agent.ExecuteBeadStatusAlreadySatisfied:
386			return nil // exit 0
387		case agent.ExecuteBeadStatusPreservedNeedsReview, agent.ExecuteBeadStatusNoChanges,
388			agent.ExecuteBeadStatusNoEvidenceProduced:
389			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitPreservedOrNone, Message: ""}
390		default:
391			// execution_failed and all other terminal non-success statuses
392			return &ExitError{Code: tryExitFailed, Message: ""}
393		}
394	}
395	
396	// unmetDeps returns the IDs of deps that are not yet closed, in the order
397	// they appear in the bead's dependency list.
398	func unmetDeps(store *bead.Store, b *bead.Bead) []string {
399		depIDs := b.DepIDs()
400		if len(depIDs) == 0 {
401			return nil
402		}
403		var unmet []string
404		for _, depID := range depIDs {
405			dep, err := store.Get(depID)
406			if err != nil || dep.Status != bead.StatusClosed {
407				unmet = append(unmet, depID)
408			}
409		}
410		return unmet
411	}
412	
413	// singleBeadStore wraps a *bead.Store and overrides ReadyExecution to return
414	// only a single target bead. All other operations are delegated unchanged.
415	// This allows the execute-bead worker to treat the forced bead as if it were
416	// the only entry in the execution-ready queue.
417	type singleBeadStore struct {
418		*bead.Store
419		target bead.Bead
420	}
421	
422	func (s *singleBeadStore) ReadyExecution() ([]bead.Bead, error) {
423		return []bead.Bead{s.target}, nil
424	}

35	The worker classifies its outcome as one of:

1	# Task Dispatch — Power, Persona, Passthrough
2	
3	> **Debugging routing?** Routing lives entirely in Fizeau. See `CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service` (in the agent repo, `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`) for the public surface; routing internals are inside that module.
4	
5	DDx invokes task execution through the upstream Fizeau service. DDx does not
6	choose harnesses, providers, endpoints, or models on the normal work path. It
7	describes the work, requests an abstract power level, and lets Fizeau route.
8	
9	## Power bounds
10	
11	Normal execution should use power bounds:
12	
13	```bash
14	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
15	```
16	
17	Power is an integer scale exposed by the Fizeau contract. DDx passes
18	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` bounds and treats them as ordering
19	constraints, not as model identities. Fizeau reports what it actually used:
20	
21	```text
22	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
23	```
24	
25	DDx owns bead retry policy. It decides whether an attempt succeeded using DDx
26	evidence: commits, no-changes rationale, merge/preserve result, gates, review
27	verdicts, and cooldowns. If a retry is allowed, DDx may raise `MinPower`.
28	
29	Fizeau owns route selection within those requested bounds. DDx may query the
30	available model/power catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold such as "top
31	models only", but it must not pin a concrete model/provider on the normal work
32	path.
33	
34	## Passthrough constraints
35	
36	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are allowed only as passthrough
37	constraints:
38	
39	```bash
40	ddx run --min-power 10 --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
41	```
42	
43	DDx sends these values to Fizeau unchanged. DDx does not validate them,
44	score them, use them for queue policy, rewrite them on retry, or use them as a
45	fallback mechanism. If the values conflict with requested power bounds or
46	availability, Fizeau owns the typed error or actual route. DDx stops on
47	hard-pin exhaustion; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`,
48	or retry in a loop.
49	
50	## Execution layering
51	
52	The intended command layering is:
53	
54	- `ddx run` — one Fizeau `Execute` call with requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`.
55	  No `ResolveRoute`.
56	- `ddx try <bead>` — one bead attempt layered over `run`.
57	- `ddx work` — queue drain and retry policy layered over `try`.
58	
59	`ResolveRoute` is status/debug-only. Do not use it in `run`, `try`, or `work`,
60	and never feed a `RouteDecision` back into `Execute`.
61	
62	## Personas
63	
64	Personas are reusable AI personality templates. DDx injects a
65	persona's body as a system-prompt addendum to `ddx run` —
66	same persona, same behavior across every harness, because the
67	harness sees a flat system prompt, not a persona file.
68	
69	### Default roster
70	
71	The default `ddx` plugin ships five role-focused personas:
72	
73	- `code-reviewer` — security-first review with structured verdicts
74	- `test-engineer` — stubs-over-mocks, real-e2e, baselined
75	  performance, testing pyramid as shape not ratios
76	- `implementer` — YAGNI / KISS / DOWITYTD, ships tests with code
77	- `architect` — opinionated on when to reach for each pattern
78	  (monolith-first, data-model-first)
79	- `specification-enforcer` — refuses drift from governing artifacts
80	
81	See `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md` for the quality bar and
82	authoring guidance. Projects can install additional personas via
83	plugins.
84	
85	### Using a persona
86	
87	Directly at invocation time:
88	
89	```bash
90	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
91	```
92	
93	Or by binding a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`:
94	
95	```yaml
96	persona_bindings:
97	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
98	  test-engineer: test-engineer
99	  architect: architect
100	```
101	
102	Workflows then reference the role abstractly (e.g., "dispatch to
103	the `code-reviewer` role"), and DDx resolves the binding at
104	dispatch time.
105	
106	```bash
107	ddx persona list                # available personas
108	ddx persona show <name>         # persona body + frontmatter
109	ddx persona bind <role> <name>  # set a binding
110	ddx persona bindings            # current role → persona map
111	```
112	
113	## Quorum
114	
115	For multi-agent review or adversarial checks, use a workflow skill composition
116	such as `compare-prompts`, covered in `reference/review.md`:
117	
118	```bash
119	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
120	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review.md
121	```
122	
123	## Getting more capabilities
124	
125	`ddx install <name>` adds plugins to the project. Plugins can ship
126	personas, prompts, patterns, templates, and workflow skills.
127	
128	```bash
129	ddx install helix              # HELIX workflow plugin
130	ddx search <term>              # discover available plugins
131	ddx installed                  # list installed plugins
132	ddx uninstall <name>           # remove
133	```
134	
135	Custom personas go in `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/<name>.md`
136	(or directly in `library/personas/<name>.md` for local-only use).
137	See the personas README for the authoring quality bar.
138	
139	## Anti-patterns
140	
141	- **Stacking power bounds and `--model` / `--harness` casually**. Normal DDx
142	  work should request `MinPower`. Use passthrough pins only for explicit
143	  operator constraints; DDx must not interpret them.
144	- **Hardcoding a harness name in a workflow**. Workflow should
145	  dispatch by power or role binding; letting each project route through its
146	  agent configuration is the point of DDx's agent boundary.
147	- **Persona for everything**. Personas add a system-prompt addendum;
148	  they don't make a bad prompt good. Start with a clear prompt;
149	  reach for a persona when you want consistent style/standards
150	  across invocations.
151	- **Persona files in skill directories**. Personas live in
152	  `library/personas/` or `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/`, not in
153	  `.claude/skills/` or `.agents/skills/`. Don't mix the two.
154	
155	## CLI reference
156	
157	```bash
158	# Dispatch
159	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
160	ddx run --top-power --prompt task.md
161	ddx run --min-power 10 --max-power 20 --prompt task.md
162	ddx run --min-power 10 --provider openrouter --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
163	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
164	
165	# Introspection
166	ddx doctor                              # environment health
167	ddx status                              # project drift / stale docs
168	ddx bead status                         # queue state
169	# Fizeau diagnostics, if exposed by the project, remain separate from DDx verbs.
170	
171	# Personas
172	ddx persona list
173	ddx persona show <name>
174	ddx persona bind <role> <persona>
175	ddx persona bindings
176	
177	# Plugin install
178	ddx install <plugin>
179	ddx search <term>
180	ddx installed
181	ddx uninstall <name>
182	```
183	
184	Full flag list: `ddx run --help`, `ddx persona --help`,
185	`ddx install --help`.

1	---
2	name: replay-bead
3	description: Re-run a previously executed bead under altered conditions (different harness, model, profile, persona, or base revision) and diff the new attempt against the prior baseline. Use when investigating why an attempt failed, A/B-testing a model upgrade on a known task, or sanity-checking a regression. Replaces the deprecated replay CLI from the old task-dispatch namespace.
4	---
5	
6	# Replay Bead
7	
8	A workflow skill for re-running a prior bead attempt under altered
9	conditions and diffing the result against the original baseline.
10	Composes `ddx try` (the layer-2 bead-attempt primitive) with the
11	output structure from `compare-prompts` step 5 (replay aggregation).
12	
13	This skill replaces the retired replay CLI from the old task-dispatch
14	namespace. The retired command silently re-ran a bead's reconstructed prompt
15	under a swapped harness/model and dropped the result into a sandbox worktree.
16	The skill formalizes the same shape: pin the baseline, vary exactly one
17	condition, capture the new attempt as a peer record, render a diff.
18	
19	## When to use
20	
21	- **Failure investigation** — a bead attempt failed or produced a
22	  poor diff; replay it under a stronger harness or different profile
23	  to see whether the task is tractable at all.
24	- **Model upgrade A/B** — replay a representative bead under the
25	  newer model to see whether the diff materially improves before
26	  rolling the model out as the default.
27	- **Regression sanity check** — replay a previously-green bead under
28	  a candidate change (new prompt, new persona, new profile) to
29	  confirm nothing regressed on a known-good task.
30	- **Base-revision sensitivity** — replay against current HEAD instead
31	  of the original parent revision to see whether intervening commits
32	  changed what the agent produces.
33	
34	For dispatching the *same* prompt across N peers in parallel without
35	a baseline, prefer `compare-prompts`. Replay is specifically a
36	**1-vs-baseline** shape, not an N-arm fan-out.
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### 1. Pin the baseline
41	
42	Identify the prior attempt you are replaying against. Capture:
43	
44	- bead id (`ddx-<short>`)
45	- the attempt's record id (the `<attempt-id>` directory under the
46	  unified run substrate)
47	- the harness, model, profile, persona, and base revision the
48	  baseline ran under
49	- the resulting diff (merged commit, preserved iteration ref, or
50	  no_changes_rationale)
51	
52	```bash
53	BEAD=ddx-abc123
54	BASELINE_ATTEMPT=20260420T101530-aaaaaaaa
55	ddx tries show "$BASELINE_ATTEMPT" > replay/baseline.txt
56	```
57	
58	A replay without a pinned baseline is just a fresh `ddx try` — there
59	is nothing to diff against.
60	
61	### 2. Vary exactly one condition
62	
63	Pick one axis to alter. Mixing axes makes the diff uninterpretable —
64	you cannot tell whether the new model or the new persona drove the
65	difference.
66	
67	| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
68	|---|---|---|
69	| harness | `--harness <other>` | model, power bounds, persona, base rev |
70	| model | `--model <other>` | harness, power bounds, persona, base rev |
71	| power | `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` | harness, model, persona, base rev |
72	| persona | `--persona <other>` | harness, model, power bounds, base rev |
73	| base rev | `--from <rev>` (or `--at-head`) | harness, model, power bounds, persona |
74	| prompt | regenerated bead description / spec | harness, model, power bounds, persona, base rev |
75	
76	If the comparison genuinely requires varying two axes, run two
77	replays sequentially against the same baseline rather than one replay
78	with two changes — each replay then has a clean 1-vs-baseline diff.
79	
80	### 3. Dispatch the replay attempt
81	
82	Run `ddx try` with the altered condition. The replay must run in an
83	isolated worktree so it cannot stomp the baseline result; `ddx try`
84	defaults to that, but pass `--no-merge` so the replay stays preserved
85	as an iteration ref rather than fast-forwarding into the base branch.
86	
87	```bash
88	REPLAY_OUT=replay-$BEAD-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
89	mkdir -p "$REPLAY_OUT"
90	
91	ddx try "$BEAD" \
92	  --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" \
93	  --harness codex \
94	  --min-power 10 \
95	  --no-merge \
96	  > "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.log" 2> "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.err"
97	```
98	
99	Notes:
100	
101	- `--from <rev>` pins the base revision to match the baseline. Omit
102	  (or use `--at-head`) only when base-rev is the axis being varied.
103	- `--no-merge` preserves the replay's worktree state as an iteration
104	  ref so its diff can be inspected without disturbing the base
105	  branch. The baseline already shipped (or didn't); the replay is an
106	  observation, not a re-merge.
107	- Capture stderr separately. A replay that errored is a result —
108	  surface it in the report rather than silently dropping it.
109	
110	### 4. Locate the replay's record
111	
112	`ddx try` writes a layer-2 record under the unified run substrate.
113	Capture its attempt id alongside the baseline's:
114	
115	```bash
116	REPLAY_ATTEMPT=$(ddx tries list --bead "$BEAD" --limit 1 --format id)
117	ddx tries show "$REPLAY_ATTEMPT" > "$REPLAY_OUT/replay.txt"
118	```
119	
120	### 5. Diff baseline vs replay
121	
122	The replay aggregation shape is a direct 1-vs-1 diff, not an N-arm
123	roll-up. Surface differences along three dimensions:
124	
125	**Outcome diff:**
126	
127	| Dimension | Baseline | Replay |
128	|---|---|---|
129	| exit | merged / preserved / no_changes | merged / preserved / no_changes |
130	| AC items satisfied | `<n>/<m>` | `<n>/<m>` |
131	| token / duration | from run record | from run record |
132	
133	**Code diff:**
134	
135	```bash
136	git diff "$BASELINE_REF" "$REPLAY_REF" -- > "$REPLAY_OUT/code.diff"
137	```
138	
139	Where `$BASELINE_REF` is the baseline merge commit (or its
140	preserved-iteration ref) and `$REPLAY_REF` is the replay's preserved
141	iteration ref. The code diff is the load-bearing artifact — the
142	verdict is downstream of "what actually changed in the worktree."
143	
144	**Reasoning diff (if available):**
145	
146	If both attempts produced session logs or written rationale, summarize
147	how they differ — but do not let the model self-narrative dominate
148	over the code diff. The code is the ground truth.
149	
150	### 6. Render the replay report
151	
152	```bash
153	cat > "$REPLAY_OUT/report.md" <<EOF
154	# Replay report: $BEAD
155	
156	- Baseline attempt: $BASELINE_ATTEMPT
157	- Replay attempt:   $REPLAY_ATTEMPT
158	- Axis varied:      harness (claude → codex)
159	- Held constant:    min-power=10, persona=<same>, base-rev=<same>
160	
161	## Outcome diff
162	
163	<table from step 5>
164	
165	## Code diff
166	
167	\`\`\`diff
168	$(cat "$REPLAY_OUT/code.diff")
169	\`\`\`
170	
171	## Verdict
172	
173	<improved / regressed / equivalent — human judgment, not mechanical>
174	EOF
175	```
176	
177	If the replay is associated with the bead's evidence trail, attach:
178	
179	```bash
180	ddx bead evidence add "$BEAD" --type replay --body "$REPLAY_OUT/report.md"
181	```
182	
183	Do not collapse the verdict to a single "improved/regressed" label
184	mechanically. Replay verdicts require human judgment on whether the
185	new diff is genuinely better, equivalent under noise, or regressed
186	on a dimension the baseline got right.
187	
188	## Replacing the retired replay CLI
189	
190	The retired command mapped to this workflow:
191	
192	| Old | New |
193	|---|---|
194	| old replay CLI with `--harness <h>` | `ddx try <bead> --harness <h> --no-merge` (step 3) |
195	| old replay CLI with `--model <m>` | `ddx try <bead> --model <m> --no-merge` |
196	| old replay CLI with `--at-head` | `ddx try <bead> --at-head --no-merge` (varies base-rev axis) |
197	| old replay CLI with `--sandbox` | always implicit — `ddx try` runs in an isolated worktree |
198	| inline reconstructed prompt | bead description is re-resolved by `ddx try` per layer-2 |
199	| no diff rendering | step 5/6 produce explicit baseline-vs-replay diff |
200	
201	Behavioral parity requires pinning `--from` to the baseline's base
202	revision (the retired command defaulted to "closing commit parent")
203	unless base-rev is the varied axis.
204	
205	## Anti-patterns
206	
207	- **Replay without a pinned baseline.** A replay against nothing is
208	  just a `ddx try` re-attempt; there is no diff and no insight. If
209	  you do not have an attempt id to compare against, you are not
210	  replaying — you are re-trying.
211	- **Varying multiple conditions at once.** A replay that swaps
212	  harness *and* profile cannot attribute the diff to either change.
213	  Run sequential single-axis replays instead.
214	- **Merging the replay.** Replays are observations, not re-merges.
215	  Always pass `--no-merge` so the baseline's already-shipped result
216	  is not silently overwritten by a "better-looking" replay diff.
217	- **Mechanical winner-picking.** "Replay produced more lines /
218	  closed more AC items" is not a verdict. The replay diff might be
219	  larger and worse. Human judgment closes the comparison.
220	- **Comparing only the rationale.** Self-narratives diverge between
221	  attempts even when the code diff is essentially identical. The
222	  code diff is the ground truth; the rationale is supporting
223	  context.
224	- **Skipping `--from`.** Omitting `--from` lets the replay drift to
225	  HEAD; the resulting diff conflates "different agent" with
226	  "different starting state." Pin the base rev unless that *is* the
227	  axis being varied.
228	
229	## CLI reference
230	
231	```bash
232	# Replay under a different harness, baseline-pinned, no merge
233	ddx try "$BEAD" --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" --harness codex --no-merge
234	
235	# Replay under a different model
236	ddx try "$BEAD" --from "$BASELINE_BASE_REV" --model claude-opus-4-7 --no-merge
237	
238	# Replay against current HEAD (varies the base-rev axis)
239	ddx try "$BEAD" --at-head --no-merge
240	
241	# Inspect baseline and replay records
242	ddx tries show "$BASELINE_ATTEMPT"
243	ddx tries show "$REPLAY_ATTEMPT"
244	
245	# Diff the worktree refs
246	git diff "$BASELINE_REF" "$REPLAY_REF" --
247	
248	# Attach the replay report as bead evidence
249	ddx bead evidence add "$BEAD" --type replay --body replay-out/report.md
250	```
251	
252	## Related skills
253	
254	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm parallel dispatch when there is no
255	  baseline, just a comparison matrix. Replay is its 1-vs-baseline
256	  specialization.
257	- `adversarial-review` — pressure-test the replay's diff with a
258	  critic prompt before accepting "replay improved baseline."
259	- `benchmark-suite` — for replaying many beads under the same
260	  altered condition (e.g., "how does the new model perform across
261	  the last 20 closed beads"), compose this skill across a bead
262	  matrix.

1	# Work — Drain the Queue, Execute, Verify, Close
2	
3	"Doing work" in DDx means draining the ready queue: pick the top ready bead,
4	run one or more `ddx try` attempts, verify the result, and close the bead on
5	success or leave it available for a future eligible retry.
6	
7	## Default surface: `ddx work`
8	
9	```bash
10	ddx work
11	```
12	
13	`ddx work` drains the queue by picking ready beads and invoking `ddx try`.
14	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`, which is the single agent invocation primitive. DDx
15	owns queue iteration, attempt evidence, and retry policy; the upstream agent
16	owns provider/model routing.
17	
18	Flags worth knowing:
19	
20	- `--once` — pick one bead and stop (don't loop).
21	- `--poll-interval <dur>` — continuous worker mode; wait between
22	  iterations.
23	- `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` — requested agent power bounds.
24	- `--top-power` — choose a `MinPower` threshold from the agent catalog.
25	- `--harness <name>` / `--provider <name>` / `--model <ref>` — passthrough
26	  constraints only. DDx sends them unchanged and does not route on them.
27	
28	## Primitive: `ddx try`
29	
30	For targeted re-runs, debugging, or running a specific bead:
31	
32	```bash
33	ddx try <bead-id>
34	ddx try <bead-id> --from <rev>      # base commit override
35	ddx try <bead-id> --no-merge        # preserve result, don't land
36	```
37	
38	`ddx try` runs one bead attempt in an isolated git worktree. It calls `ddx run`
39	for the actual agent invocation.
40	
41	## Pick by default, not by ID
42	
43	Under normal operation, **don't specify a bead ID**. `ddx work` picks
44	the top ready bead based on priority + dependency satisfaction. Only
45	pin a specific ID when debugging (`ddx try <id>`) or
46	when the queue ordering would pick the wrong bead and you need to
47	override.
48	
49	## Verify independently before closing
50	
51	**Agents hallucinate successful completions.** Do not trust the
52	agent's self-report. Before closing a bead:
53	
54	1. Run the acceptance-criteria command yourself (from the bead's
55	   `accept:` field). If it's `go test ./foo/...`, run that.
56	2. Check the resulting commit against the in-scope file list from
57	   the bead description. Out-of-scope files touched? Reject the
58	   attempt.
59	3. Read the commit message — does it reference the bead ID?
60	   (`[ddx-<id>]` or similar in the commit subject is the convention.)
61	
62	If all three pass: close the bead.
63	
64	## Close on success, unclaim on failure
65	
66	DDx try/work outcomes form a specific taxonomy. Each outcome
67	maps to a concrete follow-up action:
68	
69	| Outcome | Meaning | Action |
70	|---|---|---|
71	| `success` | Tests pass, AC met, commit landed | `ddx bead close <id>` |
72	| `already_satisfied` | No changes needed (AC was already green) | `ddx bead close <id>` |
73	| `no_changes` | Agent returned without producing commits and wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` | Leave open, **unclaim** |
74	| `no_evidence_produced` | Agent exited without a commit or rationale | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate harness/commit failure |
75	| `land_conflict` | Merge conflict on landing the result | Leave open, **unclaim** |
76	| `post_run_check_failed` | Tests or gate failed after landing | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
77	| `execution_failed` | Agent subprocess errored (timeout, crash, provider error) | Leave open, **unclaim** |
78	| `structural_validation_failed` | Result failed structural sanity check | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
79	
80	`ddx work` applies these actions automatically. If you're running
81	`ddx try` directly, apply them manually: `ddx bead update <id>
82	--unclaim` to release a bead after a non-closing outcome, so another
83	worker can pick it up.
84	
85	**Never leave a bead half-owned** — every execution either closes or
86	unclaims.
87	
88	## Testing expectations for bead implementations
89	
90	When an agent implements a bead, its output should include tests
91	that exercise the new code:
92	
93	- **Unit tests** for logic with in-memory stubs for first-party
94	  collaborators. Favor stubs over mocks; mocks that assert on call
95	  sequences test implementation, not behavior.
96	- **Integration tests** against real collaborators where the cost
97	  is small (real git in a temp dir, real DB in a temp file, real
98	  HTTP via a local test server).
99	- **Real e2e tests** at the outermost boundary. E2e tests that mock
100	  the database or the network are unit tests lying about their
101	  scope.
102	- **Coverage measurement only where the project tracks it.** Don't
103	  introduce coverage tooling just for one bead; use what the
104	  project already has. Coverage is a signal, not a target.
105	- **Performance claims require baselines.** If the bead's AC
106	  mentions "faster" or "scales", the acceptance test must include:
107	  (a) a numeric baseline, (b) an explicit boundary (what's
108	  measured, what's excluded), (c) a reproducible harness.
109	
110	## Anti-patterns
111	
112	- **Trusting the agent's "done"**: always re-run the AC command
113	  yourself before closing.
114	- **Closing on no_changes/no_evidence**: only `success` and `already_satisfied`
115	  close a bead. `no_changes` requires an explicit rationale. `no_evidence_produced`
116	  means the agent returned without a commit or rationale — unclaim and investigate.
117	- **Squashing bead-attempt commits**: the per-attempt history is
118	  an audit trail (evidence commits, heartbeats). Use only
119	  `git merge --ff-only` or `--no-ff`; never squash/rebase/filter.
120	- **Running passthrough pins without a reason**: power-bound dispatch lets the
121	  agent choose an appropriate route. Use `--harness`, `--provider`, or
122	  `--model` only for explicit operator constraints, bug repros, or controlled
123	  tests.
124	- **Parallel workers on the same claimed bead**: the tracker
125	  guards against this via claim semantics, but don't try to defeat
126	  it — each claim represents an in-flight attempt.
127	
128	## CLI reference
129	
130	```bash
131	ddx work                                    # default queue drain
132	ddx work --once                             # one bead, then stop
133	ddx work --poll-interval 30s                # continuous worker
134	ddx work --min-power 10                     # request stronger attempts
135	ddx work --harness claude                   # passthrough constraint
136	
137	ddx try <id>                                # one bead attempt
138	ddx try <id> --from <rev>                   # override base commit
139	ddx try <id> --no-merge                     # preserve iteration
140	```
141	
142	Full flag list: `ddx work --help`, `ddx try --help`, `ddx run --help`.

Use when you need to benchmark prompt variants, run a quorum-style consensus check, compare model outputs side-by-side, or replay a prompt under altered conditions. 5	
6	# Compare Prompts
7	
8	A workflow skill for N-arm dispatch and result aggregation. Composes
9	`ddx run` (the layer-1 invocation primitive) into a comparison
10	matrix: same prompt, varied conditions, structured aggregation.
11	
12	This skill replaces two retired surfaces:
13	
14	- the removed inline quorum flag.
15	- the retired benchmark CLI from the old task-dispatch namespace.
16	
17	Both collapse into the same workflow shape: dispatch N invocations,
18	aggregate their outputs, render a verdict.
19	
20	## When to use
21	
22	- **Prompt benchmarking** — compare two prompt variants on the same
23	  task to decide which one ships.
24	- **Model comparison** — same prompt across `claude` / `codex` /
25	  `gemini` (or different models within one harness) to see where
26	  outputs diverge.
27	- **Consensus checks (quorum)** — run a review prompt across N
28	  harnesses and require majority/unanimous agreement before acting.
29	- **Persona comparison** — same prompt under different personas to
30	  see how role framing changes the answer.
31	- **Replay under altered conditions** — re-run a prior prompt with a
32	  changed model, power bound, or context to A/B a hypothesis.
33	
34	For pressure-testing a plan or spec with adversarial framing, prefer
35	the `adversarial-review` skill — it layers a critic-only prompt
36	contract on top of this same N-arm shape.
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### 1. Define the comparison axis
41	
42	Pick exactly one axis to vary. Mixing axes makes results
43	uninterpretable.
44	
45	| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| harness | `--harness claude`, `--harness codex`, `--harness gemini` | prompt, power bounds, persona |
48	| model | `--model <a>`, `--model <b>` | harness, prompt, persona |
49	| prompt | prompt-v1.md, prompt-v2.md | harness, model, persona |
50	| persona | `--persona reviewer`, `--persona architect` | harness, prompt, model |
51	| power | `--min-power 1`, `--min-power 10` | harness, prompt, persona |
52	
53	If you must vary two axes (e.g., harness × model), enumerate the full
54	matrix explicitly and label each arm.
55	
56	### 2. Author the prompt
57	
58	Write a single prompt file. Include an **output contract** so each
59	arm produces structurally comparable output:
60	
61	```markdown
62	## Task
63	
64	<the actual ask — code review, plan critique, design question, etc.>
65	
66	## Output contract
67	
68	Produce your answer as:
69	
70	### Verdict: <one line>
71	
72	### Reasoning
73	<2–6 sentences>
74	
75	### Specific findings
76	- <finding 1>
77	- <finding 2>
78	```
79

**File:** `/var/tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-0248f921-20260507T043127-0ea7a081/cli/cmd/try.go`

**Purpose:** Execute a specific bead through the full production machinery: claim → execute → land → review.

**Key differences from `ddx work`:**
- Targets a **single specific bead by ID** (vs. queue picking by priority)

- `1` — preserved_for_review or no_changes
- `2` — execution_failed or other terminal non-success

582	func (f *CommandFactory) warnIfInstalledBinaryBehindSource(cmd *cobra.Command) {
583		if f.Version == "" || f.Version == "dev" {
584			return
585		}
586		if f.Commit == "" || f.Commit == "unknown" {
587			return
588		}
589	
590		repoRoot := gitpkg.FindProjectRoot(f.WorkingDir)
591		if repoRoot == "" || !gitpkg.IsRepository(repoRoot) {
592			return
593		}
594	
595		originURL, err := gitCommandOutput(repoRoot, "remote", "get-url", "origin")
596		if err != nil || !isDDXOriginURL(originURL) {
597			return
598		}
599	
600		buildSHA, err := gitResolveCommit(repoRoot, f.Commit)
601		if err != nil {
602			return
603		}
604		headSHA, err := gitCommandOutput(repoRoot, "rev-parse", "HEAD")
605		if err != nil {
606			return
607		}
608	
609		if err := gitpkg.Command(context.Background(), repoRoot, "merge-base", "--is-ancestor", buildSHA, headSHA).Run(); err != nil {
610			return
611		}
612	
613		aheadCount, err := gitCommandOutput(repoRoot, "rev-list", "--count", buildSHA+".."+headSHA)
614		if err != nil || aheadCount == "0" {
615			return
616		}
617	
618		_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(),
619			"WARNING: installed ddx is built from %s; source tree HEAD is %s (%s commits ahead). Run \"make install\" to refresh.\n",
620			buildSHA, headSHA, aheadCount)
621	}

623	func gitCommandOutput(dir string, args ...string) (string, error) {
624		out, err := gitpkg.Command(context.Background(), dir, args...).Output()
625		if err != nil {
626			return "", err
627		}
628		return strings.TrimSpace(string(out)), nil
629	}
630	
631	func gitResolveCommit(dir, commit string) (string, error) {
632		return gitCommandOutput(dir, "rev-parse", "--verify", commit+"^{commit}")
633	}
634	
635	func isDDXOriginURL(url string) bool {
636		return strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(url), "documentdrivendx/ddx")
637	}

639	// resolveAgentSession looks up a session by ID from the agent session log.
640	// Returns nil if the session is not found or cannot be read.
641	func (f *CommandFactory) resolveAgentSession(sessionID string) *agent.SessionEntry {
642		if sessionID == "" {
643			return nil
644		}
645	
646		cfg, err := config.LoadWithWorkingDir(f.WorkingDir)
647		if err != nil {
648			return nil
649		}
650	
651		sessionLogDir := ".ddx/agent-logs"
652		if cfg.Agent != nil && cfg.Agent.SessionLogDir != "" {
653			sessionLogDir = cfg.Agent.SessionLogDir
654		}
655	
656		logDir := agent.ResolveLogDir(f.WorkingDir, sessionLogDir)
657		idx, ok, err := agent.FindSessionIndex(logDir, sessionID)
658		if err != nil {
659			return nil
660		}
661		if ok {
662			entry := agent.SessionIndexEntryToLegacy(idx)
663			bodies := agent.LoadSessionBodies(f.WorkingDir, idx)
664			entry.Prompt = bodies.Prompt
665			entry.Response = bodies.Response
666			entry.Stderr = bodies.Stderr
667			return &entry
668		}
669		return nil
670	}

149			"--text", "hello",

151		)
152		// Stub Execute returns success, so the command should succeed.
153		// If ResolveRoute were called, Execute returns an error from the stub.

263	func TestDDxWork_WiresPreClaimIntakeHook(t *testing.T) {

273				mode = "intake"
274			case strings.Contains(req.Prompt, "MODE: lint"):
275				mode = "lint"
276			case strings.Contains(req.Prompt, "MODE: triage"):
277				mode = "triage"

287				close(ch)

292				close(ch)

1	# Task Dispatch — Power, Persona, Passthrough
2	
3	> **Debugging routing?** Routing lives entirely in Fizeau. See `CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service` (in the agent repo, `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`) for the public surface; routing internals are inside that module.
4	
5	DDx invokes task execution through the upstream Fizeau service. DDx does not
6	choose harnesses, providers, endpoints, or models on the normal work path. It
7	describes the work, requests an abstract power level, and lets Fizeau route.
8	
9	## Power bounds
10	
11	Normal execution should use power bounds:
12	
13	```bash
14	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
15	```
16	
17	Power is an integer scale exposed by the Fizeau contract. DDx passes
18	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` bounds and treats them as ordering
19	constraints, not as model identities. Fizeau reports what it actually used:
20	
21	```text
22	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
23	```
24	
25	DDx owns bead retry policy. It decides whether an attempt succeeded using DDx
26	evidence: commits, no-changes rationale, merge/preserve result, gates, review
27	verdicts, and cooldowns. If a retry is allowed, DDx may raise `MinPower`.
28	
29	Fizeau owns route selection within those requested bounds. DDx may query the
30	available model/power catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold such as "top
31	models only", but it must not pin a concrete model/provider on the normal work
32	path.
33	
34	## Passthrough constraints
35	
36	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are allowed only as passthrough
37	constraints:
38	
39	```bash
40	ddx run --min-power 10 --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
41	```
42	
43	DDx sends these values to Fizeau unchanged. DDx does not validate them,
44	score them, use them for queue policy, rewrite them on retry, or use them as a
45	fallback mechanism. If the values conflict with requested power bounds or
46	availability, Fizeau owns the typed error or actual route. DDx stops on
47	hard-pin exhaustion; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`,
48	or retry in a loop.
49	
50	## Execution layering
51	
52	The intended command layering is:
53	
54	- `ddx run` — one Fizeau `Execute` call with requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`.
55	  No `ResolveRoute`.
56	- `ddx try <bead>` — one bead attempt layered over `run`.
57	- `ddx work` — queue drain and retry policy layered over `try`.
58	
59	`ResolveRoute` is status/debug-only. Do not use it in `run`, `try`, or `work`,
60	and never feed a `RouteDecision` back into `Execute`.
61	
62	## Personas
63	
64	Personas are reusable AI personality templates. DDx injects a
65	persona's body as a system-prompt addendum to `ddx run` —
66	same persona, same behavior across every harness, because the
67	harness sees a flat system prompt, not a persona file.
68	
69	### Default roster
70	
71	The default `ddx` plugin ships five role-focused personas:
72	
73	- `code-reviewer` — security-first review with structured verdicts
74	- `test-engineer` — stubs-over-mocks, real-e2e, baselined
75	  performance, testing pyramid as shape not ratios
76	- `implementer` — YAGNI / KISS / DOWITYTD, ships tests with code
77	- `architect` — opinionated on when to reach for each pattern
78	  (monolith-first, data-model-first)
79	- `specification-enforcer` — refuses drift from governing artifacts
80	
81	See `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md` for the quality bar and
82	authoring guidance. Projects can install additional personas via
83	plugins.
84	
85	### Using a persona
86	
87	Directly at invocation time:
88	
89	```bash
90	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
91	```
92	
93	Or by binding a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`:
94	
95	```yaml
96	persona_bindings:
97	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
98	  test-engineer: test-engineer
99	  architect: architect
100	```
101	
102	Workflows then reference the role abstractly (e.g., "dispatch to
103	the `code-reviewer` role"), and DDx resolves the binding at
104	dispatch time.
105	
106	```bash
107	ddx persona list                # available personas
108	ddx persona show <name>         # persona body + frontmatter
109	ddx persona bind <role> <name>  # set a binding
110	ddx persona bindings            # current role → persona map
111	```
112	
113	## Quorum
114	
115	For multi-agent review or adversarial checks, use a workflow skill composition
116	such as `compare-prompts`, covered in `reference/review.md`:
117	
118	```bash
119	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
120	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review.md
121	```
122	
123	## Getting more capabilities
124	
125	`ddx install <name>` adds plugins to the project. Plugins can ship
126	personas, prompts, patterns, templates, and workflow skills.
127	
128	```bash
129	ddx install helix              # HELIX workflow plugin
130	ddx search <term>              # discover available plugins
131	ddx installed                  # list installed plugins
132	ddx uninstall <name>           # remove
133	```
134	
135	Custom personas go in `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/<name>.md`
136	(or directly in `library/personas/<name>.md` for local-only use).
137	See the personas README for the authoring quality bar.
138	
139	## Anti-patterns
140	
141	- **Stacking power bounds and `--model` / `--harness` casually**. Normal DDx
142	  work should request `MinPower`. Use passthrough pins only for explicit
143	  operator constraints; DDx must not interpret them.
144	- **Hardcoding a harness name in a workflow**. Workflow should
145	  dispatch by power or role binding; letting each project route through its
146	  agent configuration is the point of DDx's agent boundary.
147	- **Persona for everything**. Personas add a system-prompt addendum;
148	  they don't make a bad prompt good. Start with a clear prompt;
149	  reach for a persona when you want consistent style/standards
150	  across invocations.
151	- **Persona files in skill directories**. Personas live in
152	  `library/personas/` or `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/`, not in
153	  `.claude/skills/` or `.agents/skills/`. Don't mix the two.
154	
155	## CLI reference
156	
157	```bash
158	# Dispatch
159	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
160	ddx run --top-power --prompt task.md
161	ddx run --min-power 10 --max-power 20 --prompt task.md
162	ddx run --min-power 10 --provider openrouter --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
163	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
164	
165	# Introspection
166	ddx doctor                              # environment health
167	ddx status                              # project drift / stale docs
168	ddx bead status                         # queue state
169	# Fizeau diagnostics, if exposed by the project, remain separate from DDx verbs.
170	
171	# Personas
172	ddx persona list
173	ddx persona show <name>
174	ddx persona bind <role> <persona>
175	ddx persona bindings
176	
177	# Plugin install
178	ddx install <plugin>
179	ddx search <term>
180	ddx installed
181	ddx uninstall <name>
182	```
183	
184	Full flag list: `ddx run --help`, `ddx persona --help`,
185	`ddx install --help`.
186

run dispatches a single task to the upstream agent service via one Fizeau
Execute call. It is the layer-1 primitive over which ddx try and ddx work add
bead lifecycle, queue semantics, and retry policy.

DDx passes all routing knobs (--harness, --provider, --model, --model-ref,
--min-power, --max-power, --profile) directly to the service without validation
or pre-resolution. The service owns route selection within those bounds
(CONTRACT-003 / FEAT-010).

Persona injection: --persona <name> loads the named persona and prepends its
body to the prompt as a system-prompt addendum before dispatch.

Examples:
  ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
  ddx run --min-power 10 --max-power 20 --prompt task.md
  ddx run --harness codex --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
  ddx run --provider openrouter --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
  ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
  ddx run --profile smart --prompt task.md
  ddx run --text "explain this function"

      --effort string        Reasoning effort override (e.g. low, medium, high)

  -h, --help                 help for run

      --provider string      Provider constraint (passthrough; not validated by ddx run)

Global Flags:

feat(cli): add top-level ddx run command as layer-1 invocation primitive [ddx-0248f921]

Restores the documented `ddx run` command that shipped skills (adversarial-review,
compare-prompts, benchmark-suite, effort-estimate, bead-breakdown) and the
agents.md reference doc depend on.

`ddx run` is one Fizeau Execute call with no queue semantics, bead lifecycle,
or ResolveRoute (CONTRACT-003 / FEAT-010). It is the layer beneath `ddx try`
and `ddx work`.

Flags: --prompt, --text, --harness, --provider, --model, --model-ref,
--min-power, --max-power, --persona, --profile, --effort, --permissions,
--timeout, --output, --json, --project.

All routing knobs pass through to ServiceExecuteRequest unchanged. --persona
loads the named persona and prepends its body to the prompt as a system-prompt
addendum before dispatch.

Tests cover: help exit-0, harness/model/provider passthrough, model-ref
passthrough, min/max power passthrough, persona injection, and
ResolveRoute-not-called invariant.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
